transcript.text: Hi.
How are you?
Thanks.
How are you?
Good.
Where are you calling from today?
I live in Connecticut.
Oh, I am not far from you.
I'm in Rhode Island.
Oh.
Amazing.
For some reason, I thought you were in San Francisco, but...
No, no.
I'm in Middletown.
It is a beautiful day in our world today.
I'm here.
Oh, you're not far.
I'm near Westport.
Oh, yeah.
Very close.
Awesome.
Well, nice to meet you.
Thank you for scheduling some time on this beautiful Friday.
Okay.
I am Andi.

I run customer operations for GrowthX, which basically means I'm thinking about, like, people, process, and all of the aspects that go into our scope of work deliverables and making sure that we're kind of on track for client deliverables.
So, I am sort of the lead for our delivery team, which is what we call our client-facing organization.
And this role, the managing editor role, is, I think...
It is like the core of our pods, which are the structure that we use to serve clients.
So managing editors are managing three to five clients and have one to two content managers supporting the day-to-day content generation work that happens within the GrowthX platform.
So we have our own proprietary platform where we've built a bunch of templated workflows that generate different kinds of content.
So editorial, PSEO, we're working on, you know, some like newsletter and thought leadership type work, depending on like the work stream that the client signs up for.
Though right now, like our core is editorial, that platform has been in existence for two, a little over two months now.
So it's still very young.
We were using aerops before and just kind of.
Pulling in from different AI tools as we were going, but the fact that we're using our own product means that there are sort of two tracks to the managing editor role.
One is that day-to-day project management and editorial work.
So, you know, content managers are using the platform, generating the content, running a first draft, like first edit.

Managing editor is kind of the tastemaker, making sure that we're really aligning with the brand voice, all the context that we've learned from clients, and that are all the artifacts that are informing the workflows are up to date.
And so there's a little bit of client-facing work with that, but like really just project management and editorial.
And then the other side is thinking a lot about the feedback loop and how we continue to make our platform better.
So
So that we can scale efficiently.
So that comes from knowing kind of what's working and not working in the content generation.

So we're identifying patterns where maybe the output is continually missing the mark, where we need to go back and do better so that we can have those conversations with product and engineering about what we're looking for and how to make the workflows better to so that we're not doing.
We're doing less and less editing after the fact.
So that's kind of like a high level of the role in the org.
Do you have any questions before I jump into you?
Yes.
I made a couple of notes.
So when you were talking about how the pod is structured and the role of the managing editor and the role of the content managers, I understood all of that.
Just backing up to the beginning of a project, who is actually developing the brief with the client?
Is that the managing director?
Mariela described to me the various roles, and I think that there was a pod leader whose title I'm not recalling, but something like a managing director.
Yeah, good question.
Yeah.
So we have, in the last couple of months, also implemented essentially an implementation sprint before any client gets to our normal pod structure.

So for eight weeks there, the clients are with, are being shaped by kind of the best of the best on our teams, thinking about high level strategy, starting to build out our content roadmap, identifying priorities, opportunity areas.

Because at the end of that eight weeks, they are handed off to an account strategist or account director who does manage two to three pods, and kind of thinks, keeps thinking high level about, are we on track with those original goals?
Like, you know, we've, we learn.
As much as we can in those eight weeks so that we can keep thinking about like our benchmarking against what they want to achieve and make adjustments based on that.

But ideally in the strategy sprint, they've identified kind of a couple of lanes that we can go after at least over the first three to six months and ways that we can kind of tweak the strategy based on that.
So that would be, yeah, the account strategist or account director is like high level thinking about that.
But I think depending on kind of the experience level and expertise of the managing editor, that can be like a shared responsibility.
Okay.
That was my next question is at what point can the managing editor, does the managing editor typically get involved?
But I know you're still new and I know that a lot of this is not set in stone yet.
So it's interesting to hear that there could be an opportunity for someone such as myself with a lot of experience at that strategy level to potentially get involved in that.
And then.
I understand the feedback loop.
That makes a lot of sense.
I don't know if this is the right way to ask this question, but I'm just going to ask the question.
What are you actually selling the client on?
Are you selling them on your workflow and your platform?
Or is it also selling them on the content strategy advising?
How does it work?
Yeah, it's the latter.
So we think of ourselves as a services agency right now.
So we're like the first eight weeks are sold as we develop a content strategy for you.
We run calibration and generate the first few articles.
And in that time, we're also building an AI workflow that can then, you know, generate articles at a much faster pace over time.
Like, you know, so at the end of eight weeks, we can generate like five, 10 articles a week, or, you know, we can do 25 refreshes a week, things like that with our pipelines.
But that calibration has to happen first.
And so it's, it's.
And then it's delivery of the content itself.
Clients are not in the platform today.
There might be a world where they, I mean, I think we're envisioning a world where they can also start to think about those inputs and tweak them themselves.
Um, but at our heart services, if you think about it, just from like a market opportunity perspective, much bigger TAM, um, on the services side.
So I don't think we'll ever fully get away from the services work because there's just such, it's such a huge opportunity and such a demand for it.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Okay.
Okay.
That makes sense.
Yeah.
Um, well, I would love to learn a little bit about you.
mean, your resume is really cool.

Thank you.

We just kind of threw lots of ideas at the wall to see what would stick.
But in the end, you know, obviously you learn a ton when you're building something.

went to AOL, you know, obviously you remember AOL, it's still out there, but I did a lot more content and learned a lot more about, you know, content management systems and started to get more into the technical side of building content.

I was.

so I was asked to sort of.

You can't always trust what AI comes back with, and you have to spend a lot of time with human, you know, input to verify, to go back.

was, it was repeating itself a lot.
